📝 Tips for Writing an ATS-Friendly Resume:

Use Standard Headings:
Stick to common headings like “Work Experience,” “Education,” and “Skills.”

Keep Formatting Simple:
Avoid complex layouts, tables, and graphics that might confuse ATS.

Incorporate Keywords:
Use job-specific keywords from the job description to ensure your resume matches the job requirements.

Use a Professional Font:
Stick to fonts like Arial, Times New Roman, or Calibri to ensure readability.

Tailor Each Resume:
Customize your resume for each job application to match the specific job requirements.

Fast, product-focused, API-first work.

You can go from 0 → deployed in weeks.

author: Punit Kumar’s Post
text:
🐶✨ The Night Shift Nobody Expected

One quiet evening, a neighborhood noticed something unusual.

Tiny glowing lights were moving down the street. 🌙💡

People stopped.
Cars slowed down.
Children pointed in excitement.

As the lights came closer, everyone started smiling.

It wasn't technology.
It wasn't a new invention.

It was a group of tiny puppies wearing glowing shoes. 🐾❤️

For a few minutes, strangers forgot about deadlines, meetings, stress, and endless notifications.

They simply smiled.

And that's the lesson.

Sometimes the brightest things in life aren't the biggest achievements, promotions, or possessions.

Sometimes it's the small moments that light up our world.

💡 A kind gesture.
💡 A genuine smile.
💡 A loyal friend.
💡 A little puppy making people happy without even trying.

In business and leadership, we often chase massive goals.

But the strongest brands, teams, and relationships are built through small moments repeated consistently.

Small actions.
Small kindnesses.
Small efforts.

Those are the things that truly shine. ✨

Never underestimate the impact you can have on someone's day.

You may be the light they didn't know they needed. 🌟

#LeadershipDevelopment #Teamwork #EmotionalIntelligence #Success
#Leadership #Inspiration #Motivation  #GrowthMindset #LifeLessons #PositiveVibes #Mindset #PersonalGrowth  #WorkplaceCulture #BusinessLeadership #Innovation #Entrepreneurship #LinkedInIndia #Storytelling #DailyInspiration #MotivationalQuotes #Happiness #GoodVibesOnly #AnimalLovers #ViralStory #TrendingNow #Community #SpreadPositivity #SmileMore #InspiringStories #MondayMotivation

#DogsOfLinkedIn #PuppyLove #WholesomeContent #BeTheLight

author: Gautam Gambhir’s Post
text:
Most portfolios are websites.

Mine somehow turned into a code editor.

A few weeks ago, I started working on a portfolio project and had a simple goal:

Build something that doesn't feel like every other developer portfolio.

Somewhere along the way, I ended up building FORGE - a custom code editor and programming language that serves as the portfolio itself.

Instead of clicking through pages, visitors can open files, run commands, inspect projects, and explore my journey through a language designed specifically for that purpose.

What started as a portfolio became:

• A custom programming language
• A parser and interpreter
• Syntax highlighting and autocomplete
• A terminal runtime
• A file explorer
• A fully interactive editor

The funny part?

I probably spent more time building the tool than building the actual portfolio.

But that's exactly why this project became special to me.

Rather than telling people what I've built, I wanted them to explore it the same way a developer explores a codebase.

It's still a work in progress, but it's already one of the most enjoyable projects I've worked on.

If you opened a portfolio that looked like a code editor, what's the first command you'd type?

🔗 Link in comments!

author: Adarsh C.’s Post
text: Don't overwhelm to learn SQL,🙌 SQL is only this much👇😇 1. Basic SQL Syntax: SQL keywords Data types Operators SQL statements (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) 2. Data Definition Language (DDL): CREATE TABLE ALTER TABLE DROP TABLE Truncate table 3. Data Manipulation Language (DML): SELECT statement (SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, HAVING, JOINs) INSERT statement UPDATE statement DELETE statement 4. Aggregate Functions: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX GROUP BY clause HAVING clause 5. Data Constraints: Primary Key Foreign Key Unique NOT NULL CHECK 6. Joins: INNER JOIN LEFT JOIN RIGHT JOIN FULL OUTER JOIN Self Join Cross Join 7. Subqueries: Types of subqueries (scalar, column, row, table) Nested subqueries Correlated subqueries 8. Advanced SQL Functions: String functions (CONCAT, LENGTH, SUBSTRING, REPLACE, UPPER, LOWER) Date and time functions (DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP, DATEPART, DATEADD) Numeric functions 9. Views: Creating views Modifying views Dropping views 10. Indexes: Creating indexes Using indexes for query optimization 11. Transactions: ACID properties Transaction management (BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT) Transaction isolation levels 12. Data Integrity and Security: Data integrity constraints (referential integrity, entity integrity) GRANT and REVOKE statements (granting and revoking permissions) Database security best practices 13. Stored Procedures and Functions: Creating stored procedures Executing stored procedures Creating functions Using functions in queries 14. Performance Optimization: Query optimization techniques (using indexes, optimizing joins, reducing subqueries) Performance tuning best practices 15. Advanced SQL Concepts: Recursive queries Pivot and unpivot operations Window functions (Row_number, rank, dense_rank, lead & lag) CTEs (Common Table Expressions) Dynamic SQL #interview #interviewprep #interviewpro #dsa #coding #notes #adarshchetan Do follow for more such amazing stuff ♥️

author: Sai Reddy’s Post
text: Building a RAG system. Googled "best tools for RAG." Got 500 different answers. Completely lost. 🎁 One article that help me to master Prompt and context engineering: https://lnkd.in/dNqDV-2C Then I found this breakdown. Here's what each layer actually does: 🔹LAYER 1: LLMs (The Brain) What they do: Generate responses, understand context Your options: → Phi-4, Gemma 3 (lightweight, fast) → Mistral, Claude (balanced) → OpenAI, Gemini (powerful, expensive) → Llama 4, Qwen 3, DeepSeek (open-source) Start with: OpenAI (easiest) → Switch to open-source later (cheaper) 🔹LAYER 2: Frameworks (The Builder) What they do: Connect LLMs to your data Your options: → LangChain (most popular, lots of features) → Llama Index (best for RAG specifically) → Haystack (production-ready) Pick based on: Complexity of your use case Start with: LangChain (biggest community) or Llama Index (RAG-focused) 🔹LAYER 3: Vector Databases (The Memory) What they do: Store embeddings, enable semantic search Your options: → Chroma (easiest, local development) → Pinecone (managed, scalable) → Qdrant (fast, self-hosted) → Weaviate (feature-rich) → Milvus (open-source, production) → PostgreSQL with pgvector (if you already use Postgres) → Cassandra (enterprise scale) Pick based on: Scale and hosting preference Start with: Chroma (local testing) → Pinecone/Qdrant (production) 🔹LAYER 4: Data Extraction (The Scraper) What they do: Pull data from websites, PDFs, documents Your options: → Crawl4AI (AI-powered scraping) → FireCrawl (web scraping) → Scrape GraphQL (API scraping) → MegaParser (complex documents) Pick based on: Data source type Start with: Llama Parse (PDFs) or FireCrawl (websites) 🔹LAYER 5: Open LLM Access (The Free Option) What they do: Access open-source models without hosting Your options: → Hugging Face (largest model hub) → Ollama (run models locally) → Groq (fastest inference) → Together AI (managed open models) Pick based on: Budget and control Start with: Hugging Face (free tier) → Ollama (full control) 🔹LAYER 6: Text Embeddings (The Translator) What they do: Convert text into vectors for similarity search Start with: RAGAS (industry standard) Follow Sai Reddy for more such posts! author: Data Science Project Structure for Reproducibility and Scalability
text:
I made this mistake in Data Science for 4 years... Maybe you're making it too 👀

I thought becoming a Data Scientist was all about building models.

So I focused on:

✓ Machine Learning Algorithms
✓ Model Training
✓ Hyperparameter Tuning
✓ Accuracy Scores

And ignored everything else.

My models worked.

But my projects were impossible to scale, maintain, or hand over to another team member.

Files were scattered everywhere.

Data pipelines were mixed with notebooks.

Models, reports, and code lived in random folders.

Then I realized something:

The difference between a beginner and a professional Data Scientist isn't just building models.

It's project organization and reproducibility.

A well-structured project saves hundreds of hours when projects grow.

Here's the Data Science project structure I wish someone had shown me earlier 👇

Phase 1: Data Organization

📂 data/

├── raw
├── processed
├── external

Keep your raw data untouched.

Always create processed versions for modeling.

This makes experiments reproducible.

Phase 2: Exploration & Experimentation

📂 notebooks/

✓ EDA
✓ Feature Engineering
✓ Modeling
✓ Evaluation

Separate notebooks by purpose.

Don't keep everything in one giant notebook 😅

Phase 3: Source Code

📂 src/

├── data
├── features
├── models
├── visualization
├── utils

Move reusable logic from notebooks into Python modules.

This is where projects become production-ready.

Phase 4: Model Management

📂 models/

├── saved_models
├── artifacts
├── logs

Store:

✓ Trained Models
✓ Feature Encoders
✓ Experiment Artifacts
✓ Training Logs

Never lose your best model again.

Phase 5: Reporting

📂 reports/

├── figures
├── reports.html
├── summary.md

A project isn't finished until stakeholders understand the results.

Visualizations and reports matter as much as the model.

Phase 6: Configuration & Testing

📂 config/

├── config.yaml

📂 tests/

├── test_data.py
├── test_model.py

Learn:

✓ Configuration Management
✓ Unit Testing
✓ Integration Testing
✓ Validation Pipelines

This is where Data Science becomes Engineering.

Phase 7: Industry Ready Projects

Add:

✓ Docker
✓ Git
✓ CI/CD
✓ Experiment Tracking
✓ MLflow
✓ FastAPI
✓ Monitoring
✓ Model Deployment

Most portfolio projects stop before this stage.

That's why the gap between a project and a production system is huge.

💡 Resources I'd Recommend:

• Python Documentation
• Pandas Documentation
• scikit-learn-Learn Documentation
• Kaggle Learn
• StatQuest
• FastAI
• w3schoo

author: Developers Wanted: Build Products, Solve Business Problems
text:
🚨 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹-𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘀 𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟲 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟮.

I've been reviewing job postings and hiring trends recently.
And one thing is crystal clear:
Companies are no longer hiring developers just to write code.
They're hiring developers who can:
✅ Build products
✅ Solve real business problems
✅ Work with AI tools
✅ Deploy applications
✅ Communicate effectively

Here's what the market wants in 2026:
🎨 Frontend
• React 19
• Next.js 15
• TypeScript
• Tailwind CSS

⚙️ Backend
• Node.js
• NestJS
• FastAPI
• Django
• REST & GraphQL APIs

🗄️ Databases
• PostgreSQL
• MongoDB
• MySQL
• Redis

☁️ DevOps & Cloud
• Docker
• AWS
• CI/CD
• Kubernetes

🤖 Emerging Skills
• AI Integrations
• OpenAI APIs
• LangChain Basics
• Edge Computing
• Web3 Fundamentals

But here's the part most people ignore...
Many developers are busy collecting certificates.
The developers getting hired are building projects.
Recruiters won't remember your 20 certifications.

They will remember:
👉 The app you deployed.
👉 The API you built.
👉 The project on your GitHub.
👉 The problems you solved.

Your portfolio is becoming more valuable than your resume.
And your GitHub is becoming your proof of work.
The developers who win in 2026 won't necessarily know everything.

They'll be the ones who:

author: Navaneesh A’s Post
text:
AI agents are about to 𝙗𝙧𝙚𝙖𝙠 GitHub. 🤖💥

GitHub was built for humans.
One developer, one commit, one merge.

But the 𝗲𝗿𝗮 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 is here—and they don’t sleep.

When companies deploy agents that push 𝙢𝙞𝙡𝙡𝙞𝙤𝙣𝙨 of commits, branches, and merges, our current infrastructure hits a breaking point.
This huge load could 𝗰𝗿𝗮𝘀𝗵 GitHub’s servers.

Solution?

Companies are building new, scalable versions of GitHub that can handle AI‑driven workloads. The idea is to support millions of automated commits and branches without crashing, ensuring stability and fairness for both human developers and AI systems.

🚀 𝗖𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗿 (Origin): Building specifically for agent-native workflows with MCP integration and agent-optimized CI. 

🚀 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗟𝗮𝗯 (Next-Gen Backend): Reengineering the backend for high-frequency AI sessions, while also launching the 'Duo Agent Platform' and 'GitLab Orbit' for enterprise-scale agent orchestration.

 🚀 𝗭𝗲𝗱 (DeltaDB): They’re abandoning the traditional commit model entirely, rebuilding version control from scratch based on agent-conversation structures.


Three companies. 𝗢𝗻𝗲 𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗲.  

They’re not just scaling GitHub — they’re reinventing it for the AI era.

#GitHub #AI #DevTools

author: Vishakha Singhal’s Post
text:
I copied commands from Stack Overflow and memorized them...😂

But I had no idea what they actually did.

Then I started contributing to bigger projects.

Working with teams. Creating pull requests.
Resolving merge conflicts. Reviewing code.

And I realized:

Git isn't a tool. It's a superpower.
Every professional developer uses it.

Here are the Git commands I repeat on almost every commit:

✅ Initialize a repository

`git init`
or
`git clone <url>`

✅ Check what's changed

`git status`

This is probably the command I use the most.

✅ Stage changes

`git add <file>`
or
`git add .`

✅ Commit changes

`git commit -m "message"`

Think of commits as checkpoints in a video game.

✅ Push changes

`git push`

Now your code is available to everyone.

---

But here's what most beginners should learn next.

### Level 1: Git Fundamentals

✓ git init
✓ git clone
✓ git status
✓ git add
✓ git commit
✓ git push
✓ git pull
✓ .gitignore

Master these first.

Nothing else matters if these aren't second nature.

### Level 2: Branching

✓ git branch
✓ git checkout
✓ git switch
✓ git merge
✓ Pull Requests

This is where real collaboration begins.

---

### Level 3: Fixing Mistakes

✓ git restore
✓ git reset
✓ git revert
✓ git stash

Because mistakes are guaranteed.
Knowing how to recover is a superpower.

---

### Level 4: Team Collaboration

✓ Forking
✓ Rebasing
✓ Squashing commits
✓ Resolving merge conflicts
✓ Code reviews

Most developers stop before this stage.
That's why they struggle in real-world projects.

---

### Level 5: Advanced Git

✓ Interactive Rebase
✓ Cherry Pick
✓ Bisect
✓ Git Hooks
✓ GitHub Actions

This is where Git becomes much more than version control. It becomes automation.

Save this, you'll need it later (3 dots, top right)

🔁 Repost it to share in your network 

Resources I'd recommend:

- Pro Git (free book)
- Git & GitHub Crash Course by Traversy Media 
- Git Tutorial by w3schools.com 
- Git and GitHub for Beginners by freeCodeCamp 
- Practice on GitHub every day

Follow Vishakha Singhal for more !!

author: Swadesh Kumar’s Post
text: Most people think cracking DSA means solving 500+ LeetCode problems. . . You only need to master the right patterns first. ☑ Array → learn traversal, indexing, and basic operations ☑ Two Pointers → solve problems efficiently using left and right pointers ☑ Sliding Window → optimize subarray and substring problems ☑ Prefix Sum → calculate range sums in constant time ☑ Hash Map → store frequencies and perform fast lookups Now you're already ahead of 80% of beginners. The next level: ☑ Stack → solve parentheses, monotonic stack, and expression problems ☑ Queue → process elements in FIFO order ☑ Binary Search → cut search space in half every step ☑ Binary Tree → master traversals and tree-based questions ☑ DFS (Depth First Search) → explore paths deeply using recursion or stacks ☑ BFS (Breadth First Search) → explore level by level using queues And finally: ☑ Backtracking → generate combinations, permutations, and valid paths ☑ Greedy → make optimal local choices for global results ☑ Intervals → solve scheduling, merging, and meeting room problems ☑ Dynamic Programming → break complex problems into reusable subproblems That's where DSA starts feeling like a superpower. Most people focus on solving more problems. Top candidates focus on recognizing patterns. It's just: → Identifying patterns. → Choosing the right data structure. → Optimizing solutions. → Reusing proven approaches. Best resources to learn it from: ☑ w3schools.com DSA → Best place to learn DSA fundamentals interactively and fast. ☑ LeetCode → Practice pattern-based interview questions. ☑ NeetCode Roadmap → Learn DSA patterns in the right order. ☑ GeeksforGeeks DSA → Master concepts with examples and visualizations. ☑ Striver's A2Z DSA Sheet → One of the best structured interview preparation roadmaps. Follow Swadesh Kumar for more such content. 📌 Save this, you'll get better results from mastering 15 patterns than randomly solving 500 problems. ♻️ Repost it to help someone preparing for coding interviews, software engineering roles, or competitive programming.

author: Tauseef Fayyaz’s Post
text:
𝗦𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗗𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝗦𝘂𝗿𝘃𝗶𝘃𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗺𝗮𝗽
8-week plan

Week 1️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: Scalability, API Gateway, Load Balancing, Caching, CAP/PACELC theorems, Bloom Filters.

Study the basics of scalability and load balancing, including horizontal and vertical scaling, load balancing algorithms, and caching strategies.

Week 2️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: Databases, Data Modeling, Partitioning, Sharding, Replication

Study different types of databases, including SQL and NoSQL, and their use cases. Learn about data indexing, sharding, and replication strategies.

Week 3️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: Networking, REST, gRPC, GraphQL, DNS, Proxies, Websockets, Long Poll.

Study the basics of computer networking and RESTful architecture, including the HTTP protocol, DNS, Proxies, and Websockets.

Week 4️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: Distributed Systems, Consistency Models, Quorum, Leader/Follower, Merkle Tree, Consistent Hashing.

Study the basics of distributed systems, consistency models, and how to ensure fault tolerance in distributed systems.

Week 5️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: APIs, Architectural Design Patterns, and Microservices.

Study common design patterns like Publisher-Subscriber, Sharding pattern, Circuit Breaker, and Static Content Hosting. Understand the pros and cons of microservices.

Week 6️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: System Design Practice and Interview Preparation.

Practice solving system design problems, both individually and in groups. Prepare for system design interview questions and scenarios.

Prepare for designing social media, Facebook Newsfeed, Instagram, Twitter Search, YouTube, Quora, and Stack Overflow.

Week 7️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: More System Design Practice and Interview Preparation

Practice solving system design problems: Designing Dropbox, Typeahead Suggestion, API Rate limiting. Messenger.

Week 8️⃣ - 𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀: More System Design Practice and Interview Preparation

Practice solving system design problems: Designing Web crawler, Yelp or proximity server, Uber, Ticketmaster.

author: Angel Thakral’s Post
text: An app used by millions of people was built by the Indian government… yet most Indians have never heard of it. You may have used it today. Without downloading it. Without even knowing its name. And what makes it remarkable: • Uber invested ₹60 crore in it. • Paytm invested ₹60 crore in it. • Zoho invested ₹70 crore in it. • State Bank of India, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, Axis Bank, and Kotak Life are backing it. Yet, most people don't know it exists. Because Open Network For Digital Commerce (ONDC) isn't another app. It's the invisible digital infrastructure powering transactions behind the scenes. Book a metro ticket on Uber? ONDC enables that transaction. Order groceries on Paytm? ONDC may be working in the background. More than 10 million metro rides have already been booked through ONDC on Uber alone. While most companies are building closed ecosystems, India built an open network where buyers and sellers can connect without being locked into a single platform. That's why some of India's biggest companies aren't trying to own it. They're investing to be part of it. Sometimes, the most powerful technology is the one you never see. author: Adarsh C.’s Post
text: DSA isn’t about solving 3,500 problems it’s about recognizing the right pattern at the right time. Master patterns first, and speed + confidence follow automatically. 🙌💯 Here are the Top 15 DSA Patterns you need to know. 1. Two Pointers Use: Use two pointers to traverse a structure or range. Examples: 3Sum, Container With Most Water 1. Sliding Window Use : Maintain a window of elements and slide it to find solution. Examples: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters 1. Fast & Slow Pointers Use two pointers at different speeds. Examples: Linked List Cycle, Middle of Linked List 1. Binary Search Use : Divide search space in half to find the answer. Examples: Search in Rotated Sorted Array 1. Binary Search on Answer Use: Binary search the possible answer space. Examples: Koko Eating Bananas, Minimum Days to Make Bouquets 1. Merge Intervals Use : Merge overlapping intervals to simplify. Examples: Merge Intervals, Insert Interval 1. Backtracking Use : Try all possible choices and backtrack. Examples: N-Queens, Sudoku Solver 1. DFS (Depth First Search) Use : Go deep in one path before backtracking. Examples: Number of Islands, Clone Graph 1. BFS (Breadth First Search) Use : Explore level by level using a queue. Examples: Shortest Path in Binary Matrix 1. Topological Sort Use : Linear ordering of tasks with dependencies. Examples: Course Schedule, Alien Dictionary 1. Dynamic Programming Use : Solve by breaking into subproblems and storing results. Examples: Climbing Stairs, Coin Change 1. Greedy Use : Make the best local choice at each step. Examples: Dijkstra, Union Find, Graph Coloring 1. Graph Use : Work with nodes and edges. Examples: Implement Trie, Word Search II 1. Tries (Prefix Tree) Use : Tree-based structure for strings. Examples: Implement Trie, Word Search II 1. Heap / Priority Queue Use: Use heap to efficiently get min/max. Examples: Kth Largest Element, Merge K Sorted Lists Kth Largest Element → Use a min-heap of size `k` to find the kth largest element in an array. Get the Cheatsheet in your DMs directly: https://lnkd.in/e-hJY5fG #interview #interviewpro #interviewprep #dsa #coding #git #java #adarshchetan Do follow for more such amazing stuff ❤️

author: Saurabh Dubey’s Post
text:
Most people think building an AI agent is rocket science.

It’s not.

You just need 4 components and 5 steps.

Here’s the framework nobody simplifies for you:

🧠 𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻 — 𝗟𝗟𝗠

GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini - this is the decision-maker.

Think of hiring someone smart. The LLM is their brain.

It thinks, reasons, and decides what to do next.

𝟮. 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆

Without this, your agent forgets everything after every task.

Imagine a brilliant employee who loses all their notes every morning.

Memory fixes that.. it stores context so the agent doesn’t start from zero.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀

Gmail. Calendar. Notion. Web search.

These are the agent’s hands.

A brain without hands can think.. but can’t act.

𝟰. 𝗧𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝗿

What wakes the agent up?

A schedule. An email. A voice command.

No trigger = a capable employee who never shows up to work.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟱-𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 (𝗡𝗼 𝗖𝗦 𝗗𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗡𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗲𝗱):

•→ Define the task — what should it actually do?

•→ Map inputs & outputs — what goes in, what comes out?

•→ Write instructions — role, rules, format, timing

•→ Connect tools — Gmail, Slack, whatever it needs to act

•→ Test before you trust — run it in simulator first, fix, then activate

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲’𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝟵𝟬% 𝗼𝗳 𝗯𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲:

Trying to automate everything on day one.

It fails. Every time.

Start with ONE workflow.

Get value from it.

Then scale.

𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗮 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿?  𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗱𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗮 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿.

𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲:

Zapier → simplest, connects 8000+ apps

n8n → open-source, more control

MindPal → describe your task, get an agent

Want more control?

LangChain → custom logic, production-grade

CrewAI → multi-agent systems

OpenAI Agents SDK → powerful and flexible

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗚𝗼𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗻 𝗥𝘂𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗜 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀:

Start small. Automate one task. Get value.

Then scale. ⬆️

Tell me one task you’d automate first. I will help.

𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗔𝗜? 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵:

w3schools.com.com : Python and SQL

JavaScript Mastery Mastery : JavaScript and Node JS

𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 "𝗔𝗚𝗘𝗡𝗧" 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗜’𝗹𝗹 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗴𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗲.

🔁 𝗥𝗲𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗽 𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝘀.

➕ 𝗙𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗼𝘄 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗜 & 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁.

author: Good API Design Principles for Consistency and Security
text: My Understanding of Good API Design When I first learned APIs, I thought they were simply endpoints that send and receive data. But as applications grow, APIs become contracts between different systems. A poorly designed API creates confusion, performance issues, security risks, and maintenance headaches. That's why good API design focuses on a few key principles: ✅ Clear Resource Naming APIs should read like a business domain. /users, /orders, /products are easier to understand than vague names like /getUserData. A new developer should understand the endpoint without documentation. ✅ Consistency Use the same patterns everywhere. If one endpoint uses plural nouns (/users), all similar resources should follow the same convention. Consistency reduces learning time. ✅ Relationships Between Resources Real-world entities are connected. A user has posts. A post has comments. That's why endpoints like /posts/{id}/comments make sense because they mirror business relationships. ✅ Idempotency Networks fail. Requests get retried. If a user clicks "Update Profile" twice, the result should still be correct. Good APIs handle duplicate requests safely. ✅ Security First Every API is a potential entry point. Authentication answers: "Who are you?" Authorization answers: "What are you allowed to do?" Both are equally important. ✅ Versioning APIs evolve. Existing clients should not break when new features are released. Versioning allows innovation without disrupting consumers. ✅ Pagination Returning 1 million records in a single response is inefficient. Pagination improves performance, reduces memory consumption, and provides a better user experience. #NodeJS #JavaScript #BackendDevelopment #ExpressJS #MongoDB #WebDevelopment #Springboot #SoftwareEngineering #RESTAPI #Microservices #Docker #CloudComputing #Developer #C2C #Java #FullStackDevelopment #BackendEngineer #Java #CloudComputing #Microservices #AWS #Docker #Kubernetes #SoftwareEngineering #Java #SpringBoot #CloudComputing #Microservices #BackendDevelopment #Docker #Kubernetes #AWS #Azure #GCP #SoftwareEngineering #C2C

author: Aryan Gupta’s Post
text: My API handled 10,000 requests flawlessly in testing. Crashed in production within 2 hours. The problem? I used the wrong API type. 🎁 Lately, I started learning Claude skills from this article: https://lnkd.in/eSgv9QP8 APIs aren't one-size-fits-all. Different problems need different types. 𝗢𝗣𝗘𝗡 𝗔𝗣𝗜 - Public access for anyone. - Use for: Weather data, login systems, product catalogs - Example: Stripe payments, Google Maps 𝗥𝗘𝗦𝗧 𝗔𝗣𝗜 - HTTP-based, simple, universal. - Use for: 90% of modern web apps - Example: Mobile app fetching user data 𝗦𝗢𝗔𝗣 𝗔𝗣𝗜 - XML-based, strict, formal. - Use for: Banks, insurance, government - Example: Processing insurance claims, wire transfers 𝗚𝗿𝗮𝗽𝗵𝗤𝗟 𝗔𝗣𝗜 - Query exactly what you need. - Use for: Complex data with many relationships - Example: Facebook feed (only fetch what's needed) 𝗕𝗔𝗖𝗞𝗘𝗡𝗗 𝗧𝗢 𝗕𝗔𝗖𝗞𝗘𝗡𝗗 - Internal microservices talking. - Use for: Your own system architecture - Example: Auth service → User service → Payment service 𝗙𝗥𝗢𝗡𝗧𝗘𝗡𝗗 𝗧𝗢 𝗕𝗔𝗖𝗞𝗘𝗡𝗗 - Your UI requesting server data. - Use for: Every web/mobile app interaction - Example: Login, search, profile updates 𝗦𝗘𝗥𝗩𝗜𝗖𝗘 𝗧𝗢 𝗗𝗔𝗧𝗔𝗕𝗔𝗦𝗘 - Backend querying databases. - Use for: All data operations - Example: Storing users, fetching orders B2B INTEGRATION 𝗣𝗔𝗥𝗧𝗡𝗘𝗥 𝗔𝗣𝗜 - Controlled access for trusted partners. - Use for: Affiliate programs, reseller systems - Example: Amazon affiliate tracking 𝗗𝗔𝗧𝗔 𝗦𝗛𝗔𝗥𝗜𝗡𝗚 𝗔𝗣𝗜 - Secure exchange of sensitive data. - Use for: Health records, financial data - Example: Hospital systems sharing patient info The pattern: ❌ Use REST for everything ✅ Match API type to problem My decision framework: Public access? → Open API or REST Complex queries? → GraphQL Enterprise/legacy? → SOAP Internal services? → Backend to Backend Business partners? → Partner API Sensitive data? → Data Sharing API Which API type saves you the most time? 🔁 Repost if this clears up API confusion ➕ Follow Aryan Gupta for more resources #API #SoftwareEngineering #REST #GraphQL #Backend #WebDev #TechArchitecture
